title: Apache Airflow: Senior Track
description: Enterprise-scale Airflow patterns: DAG factories, KubernetesExecutor, custom XCom backends, provider development, REST API mastery, scheduler internals, version upgrades, multi-environment architecture, enterprise governance, and a platform architecture capstone.

chapters.title: DAG Factories – Generating Pipelines from Configuration
chapters.description: Build a configuration-driven DAG generation system that eliminates per-DAG file sprawl at 200+ DAG scale.

blocks.content: GridUnion Continental acquired NordGrid and inherited 60 DAGs. The acquisition also brought 140 pipelines from three other subsidiaries. Each subsidiary has its own naming conventions, scheduling patterns, and quality thresholds – but the underlying ingestion logic is nearly identical.
The Scale Problem

rows: Same factory. Business analyst adds YAML.

blocks.title: Factory architecture
blocks.content:
BEFORE: 200 Python files with copy-pasted logic
AFTER: 1 factory file + 200 YAML entries (15 lines each)
blocks.caption: The factory eliminates file sprawl. Logic in one place. Configuration drives variation.

blocks.title: The senior insight
blocks.content: A DAG factory is not about saving typing. It is about creating a platform where 80% of pipelines can be managed as configuration, freeing engineers for the 20% that need custom logic.
The factory pattern: a Python file that creates multiple DAG objects at module level from configuration.
The globals() trick registers generated DAGs so the scheduler discovers them.

left.label: DAG Factory
points: Creates multiple DAGs at parse time
Each DAG has its own schedule and config

right.label: Dynamic Task Mapping
points: Creates multiple task instances at runtime
All instances share the same DAG

blocks.title: Parse-time cost
blocks.content: The factory runs every scheduler cycle. YAML reads are fast. Database/API calls are not – use file-based config.
The power of a factory depends on its configuration schema. A well-designed schema means a business analyst can onboard a new source with 15 lines of YAML.

# pipelines.yaml
- dag_id: bronze_meters_germany
  source: meters
  country: DE
  schedule: "@daily"
  connection_id: sftp_gridunion_de
  file_pattern: "meters_{{ ds_nodash }}.csv"
  min_rows: 100000
  max_null_pct: 0.05
  retries: 3
  tags: [bronze, meters, germany]

items: Every field should have a sensible default. Only dag_id and source are required.
Use the same field names as Airflow parameters.
Include business context (country, owner) for RBAC and cost attribution.
Avoid embedding Python logic in YAML.

blocks.title: The 15-line test
blocks.content: If a new team member cannot onboard a new source by copying a YAML entry and changing 5 fields, your schema is too complex.
Templates are functions that generate task groups based on pipeline type. An 'sftp_ingest' template creates download, validate, and land tasks. The YAML selects which template to use.

blocks.title: Templates are the real product
blocks.content: The factory is just a loop. Templates are where engineering lives. A good template library means 90% of new pipelines require zero Python.
Factories handle parse-time variation (different DAGs per source). Dynamic mapping handles runtime variation (different instances per region). Combining them creates two-level dynamism.
The YAML controls whether a DAG uses .expand() or static processing. Switching from static to dynamic is a one-field YAML change.

blocks.title: Parse-time vs runtime
blocks.content: The factory decides WHICH DAGs exist. The .expand() decides HOW MANY task instances. Both are dynamic, at different phases.
A bad YAML entry should not crash the scheduler. Use Pydantic validation to catch errors before production.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class PipelineConfig(BaseModel):
    dag_id: str = Field(..., pattern=r'^[a-z][a-z0-9_]+$')
    source: str
    schedule: str = '@daily'
    min_rows: int = Field(default=1000, ge=0)
    retries: int = Field(default=3, ge=0, le=10)

# Validate each entry, skip bad ones
for raw in pipelines:
    try:
        config = PipelineConfig(**raw)
        valid_pipelines.append(config)
    except Exception as e:
        logger.error(f'Invalid: {e}. Skipping.')

blocks.title: Validate in CI
blocks.content: Add a CI test that validates every YAML entry against the Pydantic schema. Catch errors at PR time.
Factory tests verify: correct DAG generation from valid config, expected structure, and parse time performance.

blocks.title: Test the machinery
blocks.content: A factory that creates 200 correct DAGs but takes 60 seconds to parse is a production incident.
Pipeline definitions are code – they deserve version control, PR review, and CI validation. Every change to pipelines.yaml goes through a pull request.

items: Engineer adds/modifies YAML entry
CI validates schema and parse time
PR review checks business context
Merge → staging → smoke test → production

blocks.title: Template changes are high-blast-radius
blocks.content: Editing YAML affects one DAG. Editing a template affects every DAG using it. Require two reviewers for template changes.
Knowing when NOT to use a factory is the senior skill.

rows: Template cannot accommodate wildly different types

blocks.title: The 80/20 rule
blocks.content: 350 of 500 DAGs (70%) are factory-generated. 150 (30%) are hand-written. If you add 'if' statements to handle special cases, that DAG should be hand-written.
What You Now Know

items: A DAG factory generates multiple DAGs from YAML at parse time
globals() registers DAGs for scheduler discovery
Task templates encapsulate reusable patterns
Pydantic validation catches bad config before the scheduler
Factory + .expand() creates two-level dynamism
Pipeline definitions live in Git with PR review
The 80/20 rule: factory for the common case, hand-written for the unique 20%

blocks.content: Hands-On Exercise

items: Create pipelines.yaml with 20 entries (5 sources × 4 countries)
Build a factory that generates DAGs using sftp_ingest template
Add Pydantic validation. Verify bad entries are skipped.
Write CI tests for validation, DagBag loading, and parse time
Add a second template (api_pull) and verify both work
Measure parse time. Will it stay under 10s at 200 DAGs?

blocks.title: Looking ahead
blocks.content: In Module 2, you deploy these factory-generated DAGs on KubernetesExecutor – giving each task its own isolated pod.

chapters.title: KubernetesExecutor – Per-Task Isolation at Scale
chapters.description: Deploy and operate KubernetesExecutor for per-task pod isolation with pod templates, resource requests, and GPU scheduling.

blocks.content: This lesson covers why per-task pods? in the context of GridUnion Continental's 500-DAG, 4-country, 80-engineer platform. The concepts here apply to any enterprise-scale Airflow deployment.
Core Concepts

At senior level, why per-task pods? requires understanding both the technical mechanisms and the organizational processes. The technical solution is often straightforward – the challenge is implementing it across 12 teams with different priorities and constraints.

blocks.title: Senior-level thinking
blocks.content: For why per-task pods?, consider: What works at current scale? What breaks at 2x scale? What is the blast radius of a failure? What is the operational burden?
